### Source A 
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/2fbbec29-f7c8-45dd-a5e1-45fe9877397f)

### Source B
It is a paradox to brand a technological innovation as tainted goods by its very name. ‘Deepfake’ is a victim of its own capabilities. Negative connotations and recent incidents have pigeonholed this innovation in the taboo zone. The rise of deepfake technology has ushered in very interesting possibilities and challenges. This synthetic media, created through sophisticated artificial intelligence algorithms, has begun to infiltrate various sectors, raising intriguing questions about its potential impact on education and employability.

The dawn of deepfake technology introduces a realm of possibilities in education. Imagine medical students engaging in lifelike surgical simulations or language learners participating in authentic conversations. The potential for deepfake to revolutionise training scenarios is vast and could significantly enhance the educational experience. Beyond simulations, deepfake can transport students to historical events through realistic reenactments or facilitate virtual field trips, transcending the boundaries of traditional education. The immersive nature of deepfake content holds the promise of making learning more engaging and memorable.




### Source C 
However, while these potential abuses of the technology are real and concerning that doesn't mean we should turn a blind eye to the technology’s potential when using it responsibly, says Jaime Donally, a well-known immersive learning expert. 

“Typically, when we're hearing about it, it's in terms of the negative – impersonation and giving false claims,” Donally says. “But really, the technology has the power of bringing people from history alive through old images that we have using AI.” 

Donally, a former math teacher and instructional technologist, has written about how a type of deep fake technology called deep nostalgia technology that went viral in 2021 can allow students to form a stronger connection with the past and their personal family heritage. The technology, available on the MyHeritage app, allows images to uploaded that are then turned into short animations thanks to AI technology. 

Here are some of the ways in which teachers can utilize deep fake technology in the classroom utilizing the MyHeritage app.
Teachers have used the deep fake technology in the My Heritage app to bring historical figures such as Amelia Earhart and Albert Einstein to life. One teacher Donally has communicated with used an animation of Frederick Douglass (above) to help students connect with Douglass’ famous 1852 speech about the meaning of the Fourth of July to Black enslaved Americans. Another teacher has plans to use the app to have students interview a historical figure and create dialogue for them, then match the dialogue to the animation.

Donally herself has paired animations she's created with other types of immersive technology. “I layered it on top in augmented reality,” she says. “When you scan the photo of my grandfather, it all came to life. And it became something that was much more relevant to see in your real-world space.” 

With proper supervision, students can use the technology to animate images of family members or local historical figures, and can experiment with augmented reality (AR) in the process. 
“It makes you want to learn more,” Donally says of animations created using deep fake technology. “It drives you into kind of the history and understanding a bit more, and I think it also helps you identify who you are in that process.” 

### Source D 
Education platforms are harnessing deepfake technology to create AI tutors that provide customised support to students. Rather than a generic video lecture, each learner can get tailored instruction and feedback from a virtual tutor who speaks their language and adjusts to their level.

For example, Anthropic built Claude, an AI assistant designed specifically for education. Claude can answer students’ natural language questions, explain concepts clearly, and identify knowledge gaps.

Such AI tutors make learning more effective, accessible, and inclusive. Students feel like they have an expert guide helping them master new skills and material.

AI and deepfake technology have enormous potential to enhance workforce training and education in immersive new ways too.
As Drew Rose, CSO and founder of cybersecurity firm Living Security, explains, “educators can leverage deepfakes to create immersive learning experiences. For instance, a history lesson might feature a ‘guest appearance’ by a historical figure, or a science lesson might have a renowned scientist explaining complex concepts.”
Ivana Bartoletti, privacy and data protection expert at Wipro and author of An Artificial Revolution – On Power, Politics and AI envisions similar applications.

“Deepfake technologies could provide an easier and less expensive way to train and visualise,” she says.
“Students of medicine and nursing currently train with animatronic robots. They are expensive and require special control rooms. Generative AI and augmented or virtual reality headsets or practice rooms will be cheaper and allow for the generalisation, if not the gamification, of simulation.”

Medical students could gain experience diagnosing and treating simulated patients, while business students could practice high-stakes scenarios like negotiations without real-world consequences.
These immersive, gamified environments enabled by AI and deepfakes also have vast potential for corporate training.

Bartoletti notes, “A similar use case could be made for other types of learning that require risky and skill-based experiences. The Air Force uses AI as adversaries in flight simulators, and humans have not beaten the best AIs since 2015.

### Source A: Killer Robots: New UN Report Urges Treaty by 2026
The UN secretary-general has called for states to conclude a new international treaty by 2026 to prohibit weapons systems without human control or oversight and that cannot be used in compliance with international humanitarian law. The treaty should regulate all types of autonomous weapons systems. The report reflects 58 submissions from over 73 countries and 33 submissions from the International Committee of the Red Cross and civil society groups. The UN General Assembly is considered a venue for inclusive discussions on autonomous weapons systems, considering international peace and security concerns.

### Source B: Operation PEACE STORM: The Role of Unmanned Aerial Vehicles and Autonomous Weapons in Libya’s Offensive Strategy
On 27 March 2020, the Prime Minister of Libya, Faiez Serraj, announced the commencement of Operation PEACE STORM, which moved GNA-AF to the offensive along the coastal littoral. The combination of the Gabya-class frigates and Korkut short-range air defence systems provided a capability to place a mobile air defence bubble around GNA-AF ground units, which took Hafter Affiliated Forces (HAF) air assets out of the military equation. Libya classifies HAF as a terrorist rebel organization. The enhanced operational intelligence capability included Turkish-operated signal intelligence and the intelligence, surveillance and reconnaissance provided by Bayraktar TB-2 and probably TAI Anka S unmanned combat aerial vehicles. This allowed for the development of an asymmetrical war of attrition designed to degrade HAF ground unit capability. The GNA-AF breakout of Tripoli was supported with Firtina T155 155 mm self-propelled guns and T-122 Sakarya multi-launch rocket systems firing extended range precision munitions against the mid-twentieth century main battle tanks and heavy artillery used by HAF. 

Logistics convoys and retreating HAF were subsequently hunted down and remotely engaged by the unmanned combat aerial vehicles or the lethal autonomous weapons systems such as the STM Kargu-2 (see annex 30) and other loitering munitions. The lethal autonomous weapons systems were programmed to attack targets without requiring data connectivity between the operator and the munition: in effect, a true ‘fire, forget and find’ capability. The unmanned combat aerial vehicles and the small drone intelligence, surveillance and reconnaissance capability of HAF were neutralized by electronic jamming from the Koral electronic warfare system.

### Source C: The Story of America’s Very first Drone Strike 
In the autumn of 2001, however, the United States was unwilling to launch a full-scale land invasion in a region 7000 miles from home. Instead, a plan evolved to send into Afghanistan a small number of CIA agents and Special Forces in support of anti-Taliban militias, with the aid of the US Air Force. That first October night was a powerful display of coordination involving laser- guided munitions dropped from the air and Tomahawk cruise missiles launched from the sea. General Tommy Franks, who then led the US Central Command (CENTCOM), the military command overseeing operations in Afghanistan, wrote in his memoir American Soldier that the assault involved in total some 40,000 personnel, 393 aircraft, and 32 ships. But one aircraft did not feature at all in the Air Force’s complex planning: a tiny, CIA-controlled, propeller-driven spy drone, Predator tailfin number 3034 which had crept into Afghanistan some hours earlier. It now hangs suspended in the Smithsonian Air and Space Museum in Washington, D.C., its place in history assured. Yet its actions that first night of the war – in which numerous agencies in the vast US military-intelligence machine each played sharply contradictory roles – remain steeped in controversy. 

### Source D 
Human Rights Watch released a report stating that representatives from around 50 countries will meet in the summer of 2021 at the UN to discuss worldwide policy alignment on ‘killer robots’ or ‘lethal autonomous weapons systems’. In their report, Human Rights Watch expressed objections to delegating lethal force to machines without the presence of meaningful human control. Bonnie Docherty, senior arms research at Human Rights Watch said: ‘The fundamental moral, legal and security concerns raised by autonomous weapons systems warrant a strong and urgent response in the form of a new international treaty ... International law needs to be expanded to create new rules that ensure human control and accountability in the use of force.’ Human Rights Watch proposes a treaty that covers the use of all weapons that operate autonomously that includes limitations and restrictions such as banning the use of killer robots, with many claims reinforcing that meaningful human control must be involved in the selection and engagement of targets. It goes on to define the scope and prevalence of ‘meaningful human control’ to ensure that humans have access to the data, risks and potential impacts prior to authorizing an attack.

questions.specification:
**Source A**

![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/0c07a9da-2c72-4d62-adc0-1643732a28e1)

**Source B (local newspaper excerpt)** Westbridge University integrated CiteCraft, a generative writing assistant, into its learning platform. Students can paste notes, request outlines, and receive suggested citations linked to the university library catalogue. The university policy allows CiteCraft for planning, clarity and citation formatting, but forbids submitting AI-generated text as one’s own work without attribution. For assessed essays, students must include a short reflection describing how (if at all) they used the tool. Westbridge argues that banning such tools is unrealistic and that guided use can strengthen academic skills. Some faculty members say CiteCraft helps students who struggle with language and structure, while others worry it reduces independent reading and encourages “surface-level” engagement with sources. The university stores prompts and outputs for quality monitoring and claims they are not used for marketing. 

**Source C (source from Westbridge University)** In a pilot across five departments, 68% of students used CiteCraft at least weekly; 24% used it for nearly every assignment.

Self-reported study time: students who used summarisation heavily reported less time reading full articles, but more time editing drafts.

Academic conduct cases involving “unattributed AI text” increased from 7 to 19 in one semester; most cases involved paraphrased AI output.

Faculty survey: 57% reported improved grammar/structure in submissions; 46% reported weaker referencing depth (fewer page-specific citations).

Students from non-dominant language backgrounds were more likely to report that CiteCraft “increased confidence,” but also more likely to fear being “suspected” of misuse. 

**Source D (local newspaper editorial)** CiteCraft is framed as a study aid, yet it changes what counts as knowing. When summarisation becomes the default, students risk inheriting someone else’s framing of the text before they have built their own. The university’s reflection requirement sounds sensible, but it may become a box-ticking ritual—especially when coursework pressure is high. Knowledge production is not only producing an essay; it is learning how to search, judge credibility, and sit with ambiguity. A tool that produces fluent outlines and “suggested citations” can hide uncertainty and make weak sources look authoritative. Worse, the storage of prompts and outputs normalises surveillance of thinking itself. If Westbridge truly wants human knowledge to grow, it should redesign assessment to reward process, interpretation, and originality—not just polished text—and it should treat students as learners, not risk scores.

parts.content: Suggest two changes Westbridge University could make to support learning while reducing misuse of CiteCraft, using evidence from Source C.
parts.markscheme:
**Suggest (Total <marks>4 marks</marks>)**


**Answer points**


* Introduce clearer training on attribution with examples of acceptable versus unacceptable use. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* This is justified because academic conduct cases increased from seven to nineteen in one semester, showing confusion about boundaries. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Redesign tasks to require engagement with primary texts, such as page-specific citation requirements. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* This responds to evidence that heavy summarisation correlates with students spending less time reading full articles. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Require precise page or section pinpointing and teach verification of suggested citations. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* This addresses the finding that forty-six percent of faculty reported weaker referencing depth. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Provide transparent guidelines and supportive workshops with consistent staff training. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* This responds to reports that non-dominant language students feel both increased confidence and fear of suspicion. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Set scaffolded limits in early modules, allowing outlines but requiring original reading notes. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* This is justified by evidence that twenty-four percent of students use the tool for nearly every assignment. <marks>1 mark</marks>


**Awarding marks**


Award up to two marks per well-justified suggested change that is supported by evidence from Source C, to a maximum of four marks.

Award one mark for a concrete change and one additional mark for explicitly tying it to Source C evidence.


**Marking guidance**


<note>Responses must propose feasible changes that address problems identified in Source C. A change without supporting evidence can only receive one mark. Evidence must be specific, such as mentioning the rise in conduct cases or the proportion of frequent users.</note>


**Sample answer**


Westbridge University should introduce clearer training on attribution and provide concrete examples of acceptable and unacceptable use. This directly addresses the sharp rise in academic conduct cases reported in Source C.

The university could also redesign tasks to require engagement with primary texts, such as page-specific citations or annotated excerpts. This responds to evidence that heavy summarisation is linked to students spending less time reading full articles.

parts.content: Contrast the positive outcomes in Source C with the concerns in Source D about how CiteCraft affects human knowledge.
parts.markscheme:
**Contrast (Total <marks>6 marks</marks>)**


**Answer points**


* Source C reports that fifty-seven percent of faculty observed improved grammar and structure, while Source D warns that fluent writing can mask weak understanding and make weak sources appear authoritative. <marks>2 marks</marks>
* Source C highlights increased confidence among students from non-dominant language backgrounds, whereas Source D criticises storing prompts and outputs as surveillance of students’ thinking. <marks>2 marks</marks>
* Source C links heavy summarisation to less reading but more draft editing, while Source D argues that default summarisation imposes someone else’s framing before students develop their own. <marks>2 marks</marks>
* Source C shows misconduct cases rising from seven to nineteen, indicating misuse risk, while Source D argues that reflection requirements may become box-ticking rituals under coursework pressure. <marks>2 marks</marks>


**Awarding marks**


Award two marks per effective contrasted point grounded in both sources, to a maximum of six marks.

If a response references only one source, award a maximum of three marks.


**Marking guidance**


<note>Responses must clearly show differences between the positive impacts reported in Source C and the concerns raised in Source D. Each contrasted point must reference both sources to earn full marks.</note>

parts.content: To what extent does integrating CiteCraft strengthen students’ knowledge and academic skills? With reference to all the sources (A–D) and your own knowledge of the Digital Society course, consider epistemic trust, academic integrity, the difference between tool-supported learning and replacement of learning, and how assessment design shapes behaviour.
parts.markscheme:
**To what extent (Total <marks>12 marks</marks>)**


**Answer points**


* Supported use for planning, clarity, and citation formatting can scaffold writing and help students structure their work effectively. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Fifty-seven percent of faculty reported improvements in grammar and structure, showing measurable writing skill benefits. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Students from non-dominant language backgrounds reported increased confidence, suggesting potential equity gains. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Reflection requirements can encourage metacognition when implemented meaningfully. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Heavy summarisation is linked to less time reading full articles, indicating displacement of deep engagement. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Source D argues that inherited framing from summarisation undermines independent understanding. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* The increase in misconduct cases from seven to nineteen indicates significant integrity risks. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Epistemic trust is weakened by authoritative-looking but potentially inaccurate citations. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Storing prompts and outputs raises surveillance concerns that may damage learning culture. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Assessment designs that reward process and interpretation reduce incentives for replacement use. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Authentic tasks such as oral defences or portfolios help ensure tools support learning rather than substitute for it. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Integration strengthens knowledge only when paired with clear boundaries, attribution training, and thoughtful assessment design. <marks>1 mark</marks>


**Awarding marks**


Award up to twelve marks for a nuanced judgement that synthesises ideas from Sources A to D with relevant course knowledge.

Responses must address epistemic trust, academic integrity, tool-supported learning versus replacement, and assessment design.


**Marking guidance**


<note>Responses using only source material or only own knowledge can receive a maximum of eight marks. Full marks require synthesis of all sources and a clear, supported judgement.</note>


**Sample answer**


Integrating CiteCraft strengthens students’ knowledge and academic skills to a moderate extent when it is carefully constrained. Permitted uses such as planning, clarity improvement, and citation formatting can scaffold writing development, supported by evidence that over half of faculty observed improved grammar and structure. Increased confidence among non-dominant language students also suggests equity benefits.

However, these gains are offset by risks. Heavy summarisation is associated with reduced time spent reading full articles, and Source D argues that inherited framing undermines independent understanding. The rise in misconduct cases demonstrates integrity risks, while concerns about inaccurate citations and stored prompts weaken epistemic trust. Assessment design is therefore crucial. When universities reward process, interpretation, and originality through authentic tasks, tools are more likely to support learning rather than replace it. Overall, CiteCraft strengthens knowledge only to the extent that strong boundaries, training, and assessment reforms are in place.

parts.content:
**Cameras in school**

The principal at Flynn School has received requests from parents saying that they would like to monitor their children’s performance in school more closely. He is considering extending the school’s IT system by installing cameras linked to facial recognition software that can record student behaviour in lessons.

The facial recognition software can determine a student’s attention level and behaviour, such as identifying if they are listening, answering questions, talking with other students, or sleeping. The software uses machine learning to analyse each student’s behaviour and gives them a weekly score that is automatically emailed to their parents.

The principal claims that monitoring students’ behaviour more closely will improve the teaching and learning that takes place.

Discuss whether Flynn School should introduce a facial recognition system that uses machine learning to analyse each student’s behaviour and give them a score that is automatically emailed to their parents.
parts.markscheme:
Answers may include:

**Advantages** **of implementing the facial recognition system:**

*   May provide reassurance to students and parents about their safety while at school (values).
*   May provide useful information to the school that could be used to feedback into possible teaching and learning strategies (values).
*   May provide additional supporting evidence/data if there is a difference of opinions (values).
*   May reduce the need for teachers to have to register students at the start of lessons or the beginning/end of each day, ie freeing up staff time (systems).
*   May be able to create attendance/lateness reports if necessary (systems).
*   May motivate students to work harder, participate frequently (values).

**Disadvantages** **of implementing the facial recognition system:**

*   May be seen as intrusive by students and teachers (ethics, values, surveillance).
*   May be used as part of the management of teachers (ethics, values, surveillance).
*   May not pick up the nuances in student and teacher interaction, for example, the context of the behaviour may be important, and this will not be recorded by the facial recognition system (systems, algorithms)
*   Can teachers and students “game” the system, so the teaching and learning experience may be geared to satisfy the algorithms used in the facial recognition software (values, surveillance).
*   Cost of implementation – hardware, software.

_In this question it is expected there will be a balance between the terminology related to digital systems and the terminology related to social and ethical impacts._

**Keywords:** education, acceptability, cost, anonymity, surveillance, privacy, algorithms, change, power, systems, values, ethics

_

parts.content: Discuss the significance of Moore’s Law in shaping the development of personal computing devices, including potential consequences if the law’s trend no longer holds true.
parts.markscheme:
### Relevant Points
- Moore's Law states that the number of transistors on integrated circuits doubles approximately every two years $\implies$ computing power doubles every two years
- This has driven rapid development of smaller, faster, more powerful personal computing devices
- Examples of how this enabled new technologies:
  - Smartphones with capabilities rivaling early computers
  - Portable laptops/tablets
  - Internet of Things (IoT) devices
- Consequences if Moore's Law breaks down:
  - Slower rate of performance improvements $\implies$ slower development of new technologies
  - Current devices may become obsolete more slowly
  - Less incentive for companies to upgrade products frequently
  - Some predicted technologies may not be feasible (e.g. powerful quantum computers)
  
<marks>12 marks</marks> Use judgment based on relevance and depth of discussion. Ensure there is an evaluation of both sides of the argument as well as a good conclusion with a future solution/recommendation discussed.

questions.specification:
### Source A: The Importance of Data Storage and Memory in Computers
Data storage (like hard drives and SSDs) holds files permanently, while memory (RAM) stores temporary data, supporting the computer’s processing needs. Effective storage and memory are critical to speed and multitasking abilities in devices from smartphones to mainframes.

For example, an SSD in a laptop allows fast data retrieval, boosting performance for tasks like video editing and gaming.

![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/719c25e9-ea0c-4aa4-98b6-94111f69cfdc)

### Source B: Wearable Devices: Integrating Computers into Daily Life
Wearable computers, such as smartwatches and fitness trackers, integrate technology into daily life, providing health monitoring, communication, and real-time data tracking. These devices are lightweight and adaptable, making them practical for continuous use.

For instance, a smartwatch can monitor heart rate, track physical activity, and send alerts, helping users maintain their health goals.


### Source C: The Impact of Moore’s Law on Technology Progression
Moore’s Law states that the number of transistors in a microchip doubles approximately every two years, boosting processing power. This principle has driven computing advancements, leading to smaller, faster, and more efficient devices.

For example, Moore’s Law facilitated the evolution from bulky desktop computers to powerful smartphones that fit in a pocket.


### Source D: Haptic and Graphical User Interfaces in Computing
User interfaces (UIs) allow people to interact with computers. Graphical user interfaces (GUIs) use icons and visuals to make navigation intuitive, while haptic interfaces provide touch feedback, enhancing the tactile experience in applications like gaming and virtual reality.

For example, smartphones combine haptic feedback with touchscreens, making interactions more intuitive for users.

parts.content: With reference to Sources A–D and your own knowledge, discuss the implications of technological advancements in computing on user experience and lifestyle. Include examples of at least two advancements.
parts.markscheme:
### Introduction

Technological advancements in computing have significantly influenced user experience and lifestyle by enhancing accessibility, productivity, and connectivity. Advancements in storage, processing, interfaces, and wearable technology have played a crucial role in this transformation.

### Enhanced User Experience Through Storage and Memory (Source A)

- SSD Storage: Solid-state drives (SSDs) allow faster data retrieval, supporting performance-heavy tasks like video editing, gaming, and multitasking on personal computers. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- RAM: Increased random-access memory (RAM) improves multitasking capabilities, making interactions with multiple apps smooth and efficient, enhancing both professional and personal productivity. <marks>1 mark</marks>

### Wearable Devices and Lifestyle Integration (Source B)

- Health Tracking: Wearable devices like fitness trackers and smartwatches support health management, making it easier for users to monitor metrics such as activity levels and sleep patterns. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Convenient Connectivity: Real-time alerts and notifications integrate digital interactions seamlessly into daily routines, allowing users to stay connected and informed. <marks>1 mark</marks>

### Progression of Device Capability (Moore's Law - Source C)

- Device Miniaturization: Moore's Law has led to smaller, more powerful devices, from desktop computers to smartphones, making computing power portable and accessible. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Power Efficiency: Increasing power within smaller devices allows for high-performance applications on-the-go, influencing work habits and enhancing flexibility in computing. <marks>1 mark</marks>

### Improvement in Interaction via User Interfaces (Source D)

- GUIs and Haptic Feedback: Graphical user interfaces (GUIs) make devices accessible even to less tech-savvy users, while haptic feedback enhances experiences like mobile gaming, creating immersive environments. <marks>1 mark</marks>

### Conclusion

Technological advancements in computing have positively influenced productivity, connectivity, and health due to advancements in storage, wearable technology, and user interfaces. However, potential privacy concerns arise with increased data tracking and integration into daily life. <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Accessibility to learning experiences otherwise difficult/impossible $\implies$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

### Challenges of immersive media for learning

- Technical limitations and costs $\implies$ <marks>1 mark</marks>
  - Hardware requirements (headsets, sensors, computing power)
  - Software development complexity for high-fidelity experiences

- Potential adverse physiological effects $\implies$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Accessibility challenges $\implies$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Blended learning approaches $\implies$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

questions.specification:
### Source A: Article View on Artificial General Intelligence is overhyped
The quest for Artificial General Intelligence (AGI)—machines that can understand and learn like humans—has fueled excitement in the AI community. However, this enthusiasm may be misplaced.

Limitations of Current AI:

Despite impressive advancements in models like GPT-4, these systems lack true understanding and depend heavily on training data. They generate responses based on patterns rather than comprehension, raising doubts about their ability to replicate human-like intelligence.


Unrealistic Expectations

Tech leaders often predict AGI's arrival within a few years, overlooking the complexities of human cognition. This has led to skepticism among experts, who emphasize that AI is more likely to progress incrementally rather than suddenly achieve AGI.


A Pragmatic Approach

Focusing on narrow AI applications—designed for specific tasks—will yield more immediate benefits. Recognizing current limitations is essential for responsible AI development. In summary, while AGI is an intriguing goal, its realization is not as imminent as some claim.

### Source B: The Promise of Artificial General Intelligence
Artificial General Intelligence (AGI) represents the pinnacle of AI research, aiming to create machines that can think, learn, and adapt across various tasks like humans. Recent advancements have fueled optimism about its potential.

Technological Breakthroughs

Innovations in deep learning and neural networks have led to significant improvements in machine learning capabilities. Systems like GPT-4 demonstrate impressive problem-solving and language understanding, showcasing the progress toward AGI.

Broad Applications

AGI holds the promise of transforming industries, enhancing productivity, and solving complex global challenges. From healthcare to climate change, AGI could provide solutions that were previously unimaginable, driving innovation and efficiency.

Future Prospects

While the journey toward AGI is complex, ongoing research and development continue to push boundaries. As we build more sophisticated models, the possibility of achieving AGI becomes increasingly plausible.

parts.content: Explain the difference between artificial general intelligence (AGI) and domain-specific AI.
parts.markscheme:
Domain-specific AI refers to AI systems that are designed to perform specific tasks within a particular domain or area of expertise. These systems are trained on data and algorithms specific to that domain, and they excel at solving problems within that narrow scope. Examples include:

- Computer vision systems for object recognition or facial recognition
- Natural language processing systems for language translation or sentiment analysis
- Recommendation systems for products or content

On the other hand, artificial general intelligence (AGI) refers to the hypothetical future development of AI systems that can match or exceed human intelligence across a wide range of cognitive tasks. <marks>2 marks</marks>

AGI systems would possess general problem-solving capabilities, reasoning, and learning abilities that allow them to tackle any task that a human can perform. They would not be limited to specific domains but would have a broad, flexible intelligence that can be applied to diverse situations. <marks>2 marks</marks>

<note>No marks for examples of domain-specific AI or mentioning that AGI does not yet exist.</note>

parts.content: To what extent should financial institutions be required to ensure transparency in algorithms that impact credit scoring and loan approvals, considering both customer trust and proprietary information?
parts.markscheme:
Marks should be awarded for a reasoned discussion leading to a judgement on the extent to which financial institutions should be required to ensure transparency in algorithms that impact credit scoring and loan approvals. This will involve considering the need for customer trust and the protection of proprietary information.

Possible arguments in support of transparency include:
- Customers have a right to understand how decisions that affect them financially are made <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Lack of transparency could lead to bias/discrimination in lending decisions <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Transparency promotes accountability and allows for external auditing <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Increased trust in the system if the process is open and understandable <marks>1 mark</marks>

Possible arguments against full transparency include:
- Algorithms may be based on proprietary data/models that give companies a competitive advantage <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Revealing full details could allow the system to be "gamed" <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Complexity of modern ML/AI algorithms makes full transparency difficult <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Transparency has associated costs in terms of disclosure and auditing <marks>1 mark</marks>

A balanced response could recognize the need for some level of transparency to ensure fairness and build trust, while acknowledging the legitimate business interests of institutions in protecting intellectual property. Potential ways to balance the concerns could be discussed.

$$
\text{For example: }\\
\begin{cases}
\text{Revealing key factors but not full model details}\\
\text{Third-party auditing rather than full public disclosure}\\
\text{Increased transparency for certain sensitive areas like housing loans}
\end{cases}
$$

<marks>4 marks</marks> for a well-reasoned conclusion, with a judgement on the extent of transparency required, linked to the arguments presented.

<note>Mark as a whole, using the markbands and annotations.</note>

questions.specification:
### Source A: The Black Box Problem: Algorithmic Transparency and Accountability
Many complex algorithms, particularly those used in machine learning, operate as ""black boxes,"" meaning their decision-making processes are not easily understood. This lack of transparency and accountability raises ethical concerns, especially when algorithms impact individuals' lives in areas like credit scoring or criminal justice.

For example, an algorithm used for credit scoring may make decisions without revealing the factors it considered, leading to a lack of accountability.


### Source B: Erosion of Human Judgment in Algorithmic Decision-Making
The rise of algorithmic decision-making can lead to a loss of human judgment, especially in areas where subjective understanding is crucial, like healthcare or social services. Over-reliance on algorithms may result in decisions that overlook nuanced human factors.

For instance, a diagnostic algorithm in healthcare might misinterpret symptoms that a human physician could recognize due to experience or intuition.


### Source C: Algorithm Efficiency: Measuring Time and Space Complexity
Algorithm efficiency is critical, especially in processing large datasets. Efficiency is measured by time complexity (how fast an algorithm runs) and space complexity (how much memory it uses). Balancing these aspects is essential in applications that require speed and performance, like data processing in finance.

For example, financial trading algorithms are optimized to make quick, efficient decisions based on market data to execute trades faster than human traders.


### Source D: Algorithmic Filtering and Prioritization in Social Media
Social media platforms use algorithms to filter and prioritize content, often shaping users' experiences and the information they see. These algorithms analyze user data to decide which posts appear first, influencing what trends, news, and advertisements are visible to each user.

For example, a social media algorithm might prioritize posts from close contacts and trending news topics to keep users engaged.

parts.content:
**The human face of student counselling**

Many Diploma Programme (DP) coordinators are finding they do not have enough time to interview every student before they choose their DP subjects. They are considering purchasing the Student Counselling System, a proprietary management information system (MIS).

The developers of the Student Counselling System claim that, if students complete online questionnaires to determine their interests, abilities and future goals, the system can give them very accurate results, resulting in less than 5% of students changing their diploma subjects. They also claim the Student Counselling System will allow DP coordinators to be able to interview every student, something they cannot do now.

Some DP coordinators have concerns about the automation of this process and believe that advising students is something that is best done by relying on their own professional judgement and intuition.

Discuss whether DP coordinators should rely on the recommendations of the Student Counselling System rather than their own professional judgement and intuition when advising students about what DP subjects to study.
parts.markscheme:
_Answers may include:_

**Reasons why DP coordinators should rely on the recommendations of the Student Counselling System (claim):**

*   If a large number of decisions based on a wide range of scenarios are programmed into the Student Counselling System, it should give accurate and reliable results (systems, feasibility).
*   Students may be able to carry out initial investigations themselves to determine whether their proposed choices are realistic (judgement).
*   If many of the scenarios are similar, the DP coordinator may be able to see a number of students together to give general advice, making the process more efficient.
*   Students may appreciate the consistency / transparency of the information provided by the Student Counselling System (equity).
*   In many cases, the scenario may be relatively straightforward, and the Student Counselling System may be able to provide consistent information that the DP coordinators would have in a fraction of the time, allowing them to focus on the students who do have atypical requirements (feasibility, costs).

**Reasons why DP coordinators should rely on their judgement (counter-claim):**

*   DP coordinators know that special circumstances / family issues / personal information can have an impact on the decision and this will require their professional judgement, intuition, valuesandethics.
*   They may encounter scenarios that are not part of the system, which limit its usability / feasibility.
*   DP coordinators might be aware of new subjects/majors that have not been updated into the system (change).
*   Students may have multiple interests, and the Student Counselling System cannot give accurate recommendations.
*   Giving career advice is too important to be left to the algorithms that exist within the Student Counselling System (values, ethics).

_In this question it is expected there will be a balance between the terminology related to digital systems and the terminology related to social and ethical impacts._

**Keywords:**automation, change, power, systems, values, ethics, judgement, intuition, consistency, transparency, efficiency, algorithm, accuracy, reliability, equity, costs, feasibility

parts.content: Evaluate the limitations of rule-based AI in adapting to complex tasks compared to advanced AI systems.
parts.markscheme:
### Rule-based AI limitations

- Rule-based AI systems operate based on a predefined set of rules and logic, making them inflexible and unable to adapt to complex or dynamic situations that require reasoning beyond the programmed rules. <marks>1 mark</marks>

- They lack the ability to learn and improve from experience or data, unlike advanced AI systems that use machine learning algorithms to continuously enhance their performance. <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Rule-based systems struggle with ambiguity, uncertainty, and exceptions that are common in complex real-world scenarios, as they rely on strict rules and cannot handle unexpected situations effectively. <marks>1 mark</marks>

- They are limited in their ability to process and understand natural language, making it challenging to interact with humans in a natural and contextual manner, which is crucial for complex tasks. <marks>1 mark</marks>

### Advanced AI capabilities

- Advanced AI systems like IBM's Watson use machine learning techniques, such as deep learning and natural language processing, to analyze vast amounts of data and identify patterns, enabling them to handle complex tasks more effectively. <marks>1 mark</marks>

- They can continuously learn and improve their performance by ingesting and processing new data, adapting to changing circumstances and refining their decision-making capabilities. <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Advanced AI systems can handle ambiguity, uncertainty, and exceptions more effectively by leveraging their ability to reason and make inferences based on the data they have processed. <marks>1 mark</marks>

- They can engage in natural language interactions, understanding context and nuances, making them better suited for tasks that require human-like communication and decision-making, such as medical diagnosis or customer service. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain two limitations of copyright laws
parts.markscheme:
Limitations of copyright laws:

- Copyright laws only protect the expression of ideas, not the ideas themselves. <marks>1 mark</marks>
  - Anyone can use the underlying ideas, concepts, or principles expressed in a copyrighted work.

- Copyright protection is limited in duration. <marks>1 mark</marks>
  - Copyright eventually expires, and works enter the public domain, allowing free use.
  - Example: In most countries, copyright lasts for the life of the author plus 50-70 years.

- Fair use/fair dealing exceptions allow limited use of copyrighted works without permission. <marks>2 marks</marks>
  - Examples: Criticism, commentary, news reporting, research, teaching, etc.
  - The amount used must be reasonable and not harm the market for the work.

- Copyright laws have limitations on enforcement across borders. <marks>1 mark</marks>
  - Copyright is territorial, and laws vary between countries.
  - Enforcing copyright internationally can be challenging.

<note>Award 1 mark for each well-explained limitation, up to a maximum of 6 marks.</note>

parts.content: The evolution of the internet and computing has made it easier for people to illegally copy and distribute works of art. Discuss the impact this is having on the music industry.
parts.markscheme:
Some points could include: 1. Loss of Revenue: Illegal downloading and sharing of music files lead to a reduction in revenue for artists, record labels, and other stakeholders.
With easier access to pirated music, consumers may choose not to pay for songs or albums, directly affecting sales and reducing income for the music industry. 2. Challenges for Emerging Artists: New artists face difficulties in establishing themselves due to the loss of potential revenue streams.
They rely on album sales and streaming royalties to fund their careers, and piracy undermines these opportunities, making it harder to sustain a livelihood solely through music. 3. Shift in Business Models: The industry has had to adapt, with a shift toward streaming services (e.g., Spotify, Apple Music) as a way to counteract piracy.
While these services help reduce illegal downloads, they often provide lower per-stream revenue for artists compared to traditional album sales. 4. Impact on Creativity and Production Quality: Lower revenue may mean fewer resources for high-quality production, as smaller labels and independent artists have less to invest in their music.
This can limit creative exploration and reduce the overall quality and variety of music available to consumers. 5. Increased Reliance on Live Performances and Merchandise: To counterbalance losses from album sales, artists increasingly rely on live performances, tours, and merchandise as primary revenue sources.
However, live performances are not feasible for all artists, especially for those with physical or logistical limitations, meaning they are more severely impacted by illegal distribution. 6. Legal and Ethical Concerns: Piracy raises ethical issues, as it undermines artists' rights to their own work.
The music industry has implemented stricter copyright laws and taken legal action against file-sharing platforms, but enforcement remains challenging due to the global nature of the internet. 7. Potential Positive Exposure: On a positive note, free access to music can increase exposure for some artists, allowing them to reach a wider audience and potentially gain fans who may later pay for concert tickets or official merchandise.
However, this benefit is uncertain and varies widely depending on the artist's popularity and audience. Conclusion: The illegal copying and distribution of music have forced the industry to adapt, with mixed results. While streaming and alternative revenue sources provide some compensation, piracy continues to pose financial and ethical challenges, particularly for smaller artists. The industry must balance between protecting intellectual property and embracing new digital business models that meet changing consumer behaviors. 8 marks Valid points should be accepted with 1 mark for the point and an additional point for a supporting explanation/example.

parts.content: Discuss the implications of AI-driven automation on employment, focusing on the balance between economic efficiency and potential job displacement.
parts.markscheme:
### Level 1 (<marks>1–4 marks</marks>)

- Basic understanding of AI-driven automation and its general effect on employment.
- Limited discussion on economic efficiency or job displacement, with little depth or balance between the two.
- Few examples or specifics to illustrate key points.


"AI-driven automation can lead to job loss in some industries."
"Automation can make work faster and more efficient."
</commonMistake>

### Level 2 (<marks>5–8 marks</marks>)

- Clear explanation of AI-driven automation's dual impact on employment, including both potential economic efficiency and job displacement.
- Some discussion of benefits (e.g., improved productivity and cost reduction) alongside basic mention of risks (e.g., job loss in specific sectors).
- Examples provided to support points, though may lack depth or variety.


"AI-driven automation increases economic efficiency by speeding up processes and reducing costs, which benefits businesses."
"However, certain jobs, especially in manufacturing and repetitive tasks, may be at risk of displacement."
Examples might include references to automation in retail (self-checkout systems) or manufacturing (robotic assembly lines).
</commonMistake>

### Level 3 (<marks>9–12 marks</marks>)

- In-depth evaluation of the implications of AI-driven automation on employment, considering both economic efficiency and job displacement.
- Balanced analysis of benefits and risks, showing nuanced understanding of how automation can drive economic growth but also result in challenges for workforce adaptation.
- Detailed examination of sectors where automation is most impactful (e.g., manufacturing, retail, transportation) and where new opportunities may emerge (e.g., tech support, AI maintenance).
- Examples used effectively to illustrate points, with insights into both the positive impacts (e.g., efficiency, growth in new sectors) and negative impacts (e.g., job displacement, need for reskilling).

$$
\text{Economic Efficiency} \leftrightarrow \text{Job Displacement}
$$


"AI-driven automation enhances economic efficiency by reducing labor costs and increasing productivity, allowing companies to produce goods and services at lower prices, potentially increasing consumer access."
"However, automation can displace jobs in sectors where repetitive tasks are common. For instance, autonomous vehicles could reduce the demand for truck drivers, and robotic process automation could decrease the need for administrative roles."
"There is also a shift in job creation, with new roles emerging in AI development, maintenance, and oversight, but this requires significant retraining and may not offset losses in low-skill jobs."
</commonMistake>

<examTip>Balancing these effects is difficult, as job displacement impacts socio-economic stability. Policymakers might need to consider interventions such as retraining programs, universal basic income, or support for sectors heavily affected by automation.</examTip>

questions.specification:
**Source A** 
 
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/75f54412-20c4-4551-a3fa-66f9e52e7df4)
 
 **Source B (excerpt from Riverton’s website)**
 
 Riverton Health introduced PulsePath to support patients with heart failure after hospital discharge. Patients receive a connected scale and blood pressure cuff, and an app prompts daily readings. The system flags abnormal patterns (for example, sudden weight gain indicating fluid retention) and sends alerts to a nurse dashboard. Nurses can message patients, arrange medication changes approved by clinicians, or book urgent appointments. Riverton Health says PulsePath reduces preventable readmissions and helps patients notice worsening symptoms sooner. Participation is optional, but patients who enrol must agree to data collection for clinical care and service improvement. The hospital states that data are encrypted and that only the care team can view identifiable records. Some patients appreciate reassurance, while others report anxiety from frequent notifications and worry about being judged for missed readings. 
 
 **Source C (same as above)** 
 
 Readmission within 30 days fell from 16% to 12% among PulsePath participants; non-participants remained at 16%.

Adherence: 73% submitted readings at least 5 days/week; 19% averaged fewer than 3 days/week.

The most common alert trigger was weight gain (41% of alerts), followed by high blood pressure (28%).

Staff workload shifted: nurse phone calls increased 23%, while routine in-person checkups decreased 15%.

Equity concern: participation was lower among patients without reliable home internet and among those with limited digital literacy.

Patient feedback: many valued “early warning,” but a sizeable minority described feeling “constantly monitored.” 

**Source D (sourced from Internet blog)** PulsePath looks like modern care, but it also offloads responsibility onto patients’ homes. A drop in readmissions is promising, yet the system creates a new health experience: daily measurements, algorithmic flags, and the psychological pressure of compliance. Not everyone can perform “good patienthood” through an app. People with unstable housing, low digital literacy, or caregiving burdens may be excluded or labelled non-adherent. Meanwhile, hospitals can claim efficiency while nurses absorb more emotionally intense phone calls. There is also a privacy dilemma hiding behind reassuring words like “encrypted”: intimate health patterns are now continuously produced, stored, and potentially repurposed for “service improvement.” Remote monitoring can be humane when it is supportive, optional, and resourced. It becomes harmful when it is treated as a substitute for accessible, relational healthcare.

parts.content: Distinguish between health benefits and potential health harms of PulsePath, with reference to Source B.
parts.markscheme:
**Distinguish (Total <marks>4 marks</marks>)**


**Answer points**


* Earlier detection of worsening symptoms such as sudden weight gain indicating fluid retention, enabling timely medical intervention. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Alerts enable faster nurse intervention through messaging, medication changes approved by clinicians, or urgent appointments when abnormalities are detected. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Reduction of preventable readmissions as claimed by the hospital, suggesting improved management of heart failure after discharge. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Patient reassurance from continuous monitoring, with some patients appreciating the peace of mind provided by the system. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Anxiety caused by frequent notifications, with some patients feeling stressed by the volume of alerts and reminders. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Worry about being judged for missed readings, creating pressure and potential feelings of failure or guilt among patients who struggle with compliance. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Privacy and consent concerns due to the requirement that enrolling patients must agree to data collection for clinical care and service improvement purposes. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Burden shift to patients through the requirement for daily measurement work, transferring healthcare tasks from institutional settings to the home. <marks>1 mark</marks>


**Awarding marks**


Award up to two marks for clearly identified health benefits from Source B.

Award up to two marks for clearly identified potential health harms from Source B.

Maximum four marks total, requiring at least one benefit and one harm explicitly linked to Source B.


**Marking guidance**


<note>Responses that only describe benefits or only harms can earn a maximum of two marks. Benefits and harms must be clearly separated or categorised to fulfil the distinguish command term. Statements must be grounded in Source B rather than general speculation about remote monitoring systems.</note>


**Sample answer**


PulsePath offers health benefits through earlier detection of worsening symptoms, such as sudden weight gain, which alerts the healthcare team to potential problems before they become emergencies. The system also enables faster nurse intervention through messaging, medication changes, or urgent appointments, and some patients value the reassurance provided by continuous monitoring.

However, potential health harms include anxiety caused by frequent notifications, which can create stress and constant worry. Some patients also feel pressure about being judged for missed readings, and there are privacy concerns because enrolment requires agreeing to ongoing data collection beyond immediate care.

parts.content: Compare what Source C and Source D suggest about how PulsePath affects patients and healthcare staff.
parts.markscheme:
**Compare (Total <marks>6 marks</marks>)**


**Answer points**


* Both Source C and Source D acknowledge reduced readmissions among PulsePath participants, with Source C reporting a decrease from 16 percent to 12 percent within 30 days, and Source D describing this drop as promising. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Source C reports that many patients value early warning while a sizeable minority felt constantly monitored, whereas Source D frames the monitoring experience as psychological pressure and a demand to perform good patienthood through an app. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Source C explicitly identifies lower participation among patients without reliable home internet and limited digital literacy as an equity concern, while Source D expands this exclusion to people with unstable housing, caregiving burdens, and the risk of being labelled non-adherent. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Source C quantifies the staff workload shift with nurse phone calls increasing 23 percent and routine in-person checkups decreasing 15 percent, whereas Source D interprets this as nurses absorbing more emotionally intense calls while hospitals claim efficiency gains. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Source C cites patient feedback describing feeling constantly monitored but provides limited detail on privacy implications, whereas Source D foregrounds privacy dilemmas despite encryption claims and fears of data repurposing. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Both sources acknowledge that PulsePath changes the healthcare experience, with Source C reporting 73 percent adherence for readings at least five days per week and Source D questioning whether everyone can meet these expectations. <marks>1 mark</marks>


**Awarding marks**


Award up to six marks for effective comparisons that show similarities or differences between Source C and Source D, with consistent reference to both sources.

Award two marks for each effective comparison point that clearly links both sources.

Responses using only one source can earn a maximum of three marks.

Responses discussing sources separately without comparison can earn a maximum of four marks.


**Marking guidance**


<note>The compare command term requires explicit linking between what the two sources say about the same aspect of PulsePath. Listing points from each source without drawing connections does not meet the requirement.</note>


**Sample answer**


Both Source C and Source D recognise that PulsePath reduces readmissions, with Source C providing statistical evidence and Source D describing the reduction as promising. However, they differ in how they frame the patient experience, as Source C notes mixed feedback while Source D interprets monitoring as psychological pressure.

On equity, Source C focuses on digital access and literacy, whereas Source D broadens this to include housing instability and caregiving demands. Similarly, Source C presents workload changes as efficiency gains, while Source D highlights the emotional labour absorbed by nurses and raises deeper concerns about privacy and expectations placed on patients.

parts.content: Evaluate whether PulsePath improves healthcare overall. With reference to all the sources (A–D) and your own knowledge of the Digital Society course, consider effectiveness, mental well-being, privacy and consent, digital divides in health access, and how digital systems redistribute care work.
parts.markscheme:
**Evaluate (Total <marks>12 marks</marks>)**


**Answer points**


* PulsePath reduces readmissions among participants, with Source C reporting a decrease from 16 percent to 12 percent within 30 days. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Early detection through pattern flagging enables timely medication changes and appointments, potentially preventing emergencies. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Some patients value reassurance and peace of mind from continuous monitoring, as shown in Sources B and C. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Anxiety and stress result from frequent notifications and constant monitoring, negatively affecting mental well-being. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Compliance pressure creates fear of judgement for missed readings, as emphasised in Source D. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Digital divides limit participation for patients without reliable internet or digital literacy, noted in Sources C and D. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Care work is redistributed to patients through daily measurement requirements. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Nurse workload shifts toward increased and emotionally intense calls, despite reduced in-person visits. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Privacy and consent concerns arise due to required data collection for service improvement. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Informed consent is difficult in continuous monitoring systems where future data use may be unclear. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Algorithmic alert fatigue can reduce effectiveness over time. <marks>1 mark</marks>
* Human-in-the-loop oversight is essential so automated alerts support rather than replace clinical judgement. <marks>1 mark</marks>


**Awarding marks**


Award up to twelve marks for a balanced evaluation that reaches a supported conclusion about whether PulsePath improves healthcare overall.

Responses must address effectiveness, mental well-being, privacy and consent, equity, and redistribution of care work.

Evidence from all sources should be integrated with relevant course knowledge.


**Marking guidance**


<note>Responses using only source material or only own knowledge can earn a maximum of eight marks. Higher marks require synthesis and a clear evaluative conclusion.</note>


**Sample answer**


PulsePath improves some healthcare outcomes by reducing readmissions among participants, with Source C reporting a fall from 16 percent to 12 percent within 30 days. Earlier detection through automated pattern flagging allows nurses to intervene with medication changes or appointments, which can prevent emergencies and reassure some patients, as shown in Sources B and C.

However, these benefits are accompanied by significant drawbacks. Frequent notifications and constant monitoring can cause anxiety, while pressure to comply with daily readings creates stress and fear of judgement, as highlighted in Source D. Digital divides further limit who can benefit, excluding patients without reliable internet, stable housing, or digital skills.

The system also redistributes care work onto patients and increases emotionally intense labour for nurses. Privacy and consent concerns persist because data collection extends beyond immediate care. Overall, PulsePath improves healthcare for some patients but risks increasing inequality, psychological burden, and privacy concerns, meaning it does not unambiguously improve healthcare for everyone.

### AI and Transparency in Decision-Making
AI is used in critical areas such as finance, healthcare, and criminal justice, where decision-making transparency is crucial. ""Black box"" algorithms, however, lack transparency, making it difficult for users to understand or challenge AI-driven decisions, which can affect trust and fairness.

parts.content: To what extent should transparency be a priority in AI-driven decision-making, particularly in high-stakes areas like finance and criminal justice?
parts.markscheme:
### Level 1 (<marks>1–4 marks</marks>)

- Basic understanding of transparency in AI.
- Limited discussion on why transparency might be important, with minimal reference to high-stakes areas like finance and criminal justice.
- Few or no examples to support points.

Example Points:

- "Transparency is needed to see how AI decisions are made."
- "It is important in high-stakes areas like finance."

### Level 2 (<marks>5–8 marks</marks>)

- Clear explanation of the concept of transparency and its importance in AI decision-making.
- Some discussion of high-stakes areas (finance, criminal justice), mentioning the potential consequences of opaque AI systems.
- Basic mention of risks in not prioritizing transparency, such as accountability and fairness issues.
- Some examples to illustrate key points, though may lack depth.

Example Points:

- "Transparency in AI is crucial for accountability, especially in areas like criminal justice where lives can be directly affected."
- "In finance, a lack of transparency could lead to unfair credit decisions, impacting people's financial stability."
- Examples may include basic references to credit scoring or criminal sentencing decisions influenced by AI without deep analysis.

### Level 3 (<marks>9–12 marks</marks>)

- In-depth evaluation of the necessity of transparency in AI, particularly in high-stakes fields.
- Comprehensive discussion of ethical and practical implications, including how transparency supports accountability, trust, and fairness.
- Detailed analysis of risks associated with opaque AI systems, such as potential bias, wrongful decisions, and the challenges to correcting errors without transparency.
- Balanced argument with consideration of challenges to implementing transparency, like trade-offs with proprietary information or the complexity of AI models.
- Use of specific examples to illustrate implications in both finance (e.g., credit scoring or loan approvals) and criminal justice (e.g., sentencing algorithms or predictive policing).

Example Points:

- "Transparency in AI decision-making is essential in high-stakes areas like finance and criminal justice, where decisions have far-reaching impacts on individuals' lives. It ensures that affected parties can understand, contest, and seek recourse for decisions that impact them."
- "In criminal justice, lack of transparency in AI-driven sentencing algorithms can lead to biased outcomes without accountability, eroding public trust. Transparency is needed to scrutinize whether the system perpetuates existing biases, particularly against marginalized groups."
- "In finance, opaque algorithms used in credit scoring can disadvantage certain applicants without providing a clear reason, limiting access to financial opportunities."
- <note>Challenges: Achieving transparency can be difficult when models are complex or proprietary, but efforts like explainable AI and regulatory oversight could help balance transparency with innovation.</note>

parts.content: Discuss the ethical implications of user data collection and surveillance on social networks, focusing on privacy, security, and consent.
parts.markscheme:
### Privacy and consent <marks>4 marks</marks>
- User data collection often happens without explicit consent or transparency
- Privacy concerns over personal data being collected, shared, and used for purposes not intended by users
- Lack of control over how personal data is used and disseminated
- Potential for data breaches and unauthorized access to sensitive information

### Security risks <marks>3 marks</marks>
- Collection of user data creates security vulnerabilities and risks of hacking/data breaches
- Centralized data storage makes platforms an attractive target for cyber attacks
- Stolen data can be used for identity theft, fraud, or other malicious activities
$$
\text{Risk} \propto \text{Amount of data collected}
$$

### Surveillance and social control <marks>3 marks</marks>
- User data enables mass surveillance and monitoring of online activities
- Potential for data to be used for social control, manipulation, or discrimination
- Chilling effects on free speech and expression due to perceived monitoring
- Asymmetry of power between platforms with user data and individual users

### Ethical considerations <marks>2 marks</marks>
- Lack of transparency and informed consent violates ethical principles
- User autonomy and right to privacy are undermined
- Utilitarian arguments about benefits must be weighed against ethical costs
- Need for stronger data protection laws and user-centric data governance models

**Technology disruption in Orams Academy**

Many schools use virtual learning environments (VLEs), such as Firefly, PowerSchool Learning, Schoology, Final Site LMS, and Google Classroom. The VLE is used for document storage, lesson content, and homework management as well as for communication between the school, students and parents.

In addition to the VLE, collaborative cloud-based online tools, such as Google G Suite and Microsoft Office 365, are used by students and teachers.

Recently, Daniela Stark, the principal at Orams Academy, has become concerned that using only one VLE may not prove to be as beneficial as originally thought. She has heard that some teachers and students are also successfully using social media tools, such as Facebook, Twitter and Instagram, to share their learning. She has also been told that the lack of bandwidth that the school experiences at times can cause problems.

Daniela is considering an alternative approach, allowing teachers and students to choose the most appropriate tools to manage their learning.

parts.content:
The increasing use of cloud-based applications and online collaborative tools has led to Orams Academy introducing an acceptableuse policy for students and teachers.

Explain**three** reasons why Orams Academy decided to introduce an acceptable use policy.
parts.markscheme:
_Answers may include:_

*   To establish a philosophy of Orams Academy relating to the use of online environments…
*   which acts as a guiding statement and provides the overarching principles for online behaviour (values).  
    

*   To establish a code of conduct (values)…
*   that provides clear and unambiguous directions about how staff and students should act while online / using appropriate language in text chats / avoiding bullying behaviour / avoiding copying or plagiarism.  
      
    

*   To outline sanctions…
*   that would be imposed if staff or students did not adhere to the code of conduct (values).  
      
    

*   To establish a disclaimer…
*   so that Orams Academy is not responsible for the actions of the staff or students if an illegal activity is carried out / managers at Orams Academy are able to supervise everything that is posted onto the VLE / managers at Orams Academy would be able to read students’ messages (spaces).  
      
    

*   To establish data privacy and protection guidelines (security)…
*   so it will be transparent who owns the data created in the VLE, for how long, and what type of data is collected by the VLE servers (systems).

_Award **\[1\]** for each reason given and an additional **\[1\]** for a development of this reason up to a maximum of **\[2\]** marks._

_Mark as **\[2\]** \+ **\[2\]** \+ **\[2\]**._

_In part (c) of this question it is expected there will be a balance between the terminology related to digital systems and the terminology related to social and ethical impacts._

**Keywords:** cloud, policy, security, ubiquity, digital media, remote learning, change, spaces, systems, values

parts.content: Discuss whether Daniela should make every teacher at Orams Academy use the same learning platform **or** allow each teacher to choose their own preferred learning management approach.
parts.markscheme:
_Answers may include:_

**A single learning platform:**

*   This means that all of the information is centralized and can be easily accessed by staff, students and parents (systems).
*   The training requirements for staff are reduced, as they only have to use one system (systems).
*   It prevents the use of a range of formats that may be incompatible, meaning that resources cannot be shared, or may need adapting to work on a different platform (systems).
*   Having a common learning platform would make it convenient for the IT department to take backup/restore and roll over data to new academic year (systems).
*   It makes it easier for senior managers to monitor the actions of staff, view lessons, etc., for consistency (values, power).
*   Can be integrated into any acceptable use policy or training requirements (values).
*   May be costly and based on proprietary software, meaning that fixes could be expensive and take a long time to happen (change).
*   If the single platform is inaccessible for any reason, all learning content would be inaccessible (systems).

**Teachers choosing their own preferred learning management approach:**

*   allows teachers to use their expertise of particular tools
*   allows teachers to feel they are being allowed to use their creativity and innovative approach to the teaching and learning (power)
*   may integrate with social media tools that may be blocked by the school and provide a better user experience
*   may provide better functionality than a “one size fits all” approach
*   may lead to staff or students working outside an acceptable use policy / using the sites inappropriately
*   the training requirements for staff will be increased if the staff are unfamiliar with the platforms / training will only occur on an _ad hoc_ basis / training might be decreased since staff are already familiar with the tools they choose to use
*   expertise of a platform may depend on particular teachers – if they leave, then no one may be able to support students with this tool (systems).

**Keywords:** business, learning, education, training, judgement, platform, monitoring, acceptable use policy, interoperability, policy, change, systems, values

questions.specification:
### Source A: The Evolution and Impact of Different Types of Computers
Computers have diversified significantly, ranging from large-scale mainframes that handle intensive processing tasks to wearable devices like fitness trackers that support everyday activities. Each type serves unique functions across industries.

For example, mainframe computers power large databases and transactions in banking, processing millions of transactions in seconds. Smart devices, such as mobile phones, have also transformed digital communication, offering constant connectivity to the internet and applications.

### Source B: Components of a Computer: Hardware, Interfaces, and Software
A computer’s functionality relies on the interplay of hardware, interfaces, and software. The central processing unit (CPU) acts as the “brain” of the system, while memory stores data temporarily and storage components hold data permanently. User interfaces enable interaction, and software executes tasks and provides structure.

For instance, a smartphone’s CPU works in tandem with its operating system and touchscreen to enable fast user responses and efficient multitasking, providing a seamless experience in everyday tasks.


### Source C: Computer Coding and Programming Languages
Programming languages allow humans to communicate instructions to computers. From foundational languages like Fortran and C to modern languages like Python and JavaScript, each has specific rules and structures. Coding’s flexibility allows it to be applied in diverse fields, from software development to artificial intelligence.

For instance, Python’s readability has made it a popular choice in data science, allowing researchers to analyze data and create machine learning algorithms effectively.


### Source D: The Evolution of Computing: From First Generation to Quantum Computing

parts.content: With reference to Sources A–D and your own knowledge, evaluate how the evolution of computers has impacted modern industries. Include examples from at least two industries.
parts.markscheme:
Sample:
### Introduction

The evolution of computers—from mainframes to quantum technology—has drastically impacted industries, enhancing productivity, accuracy, and innovation across various sectors.

### Industry Examples

#### Finance

Mainframe computers support extensive data processing, allowing banks to conduct high-frequency transactions and manage customer databases efficiently. Advanced algorithms further enable real-time trading, risk assessment, and fraud detection.

#### Healthcare

The rise of wearable computers has enabled continuous patient monitoring, allowing healthcare providers to gather data on vital signs and activity levels for better patient care. In addition, advancements in machine learning and Python applications support diagnostic tools that analyze patient data, aiding in early detection and treatment planning.

### Technological Evolution and Impact

#### Generational Improvements

The shift from transistors to microprocessors and, potentially, quantum computing has led to faster, more efficient devices, opening up new possibilities for automation, data analysis, and machine learning.

#### Programming and Software

The evolution of programming languages (e.g., from Fortran to Python) has expanded accessibility, enabling industries to develop more complex applications tailored to their needs, such as AI-driven diagnostics in healthcare and financial forecasting tools.

### Conclusion

The continuous evolution of computing technology has enabled industries to streamline operations, achieve higher accuracy, and unlock new innovation areas, demonstrating the transformative power of computer advancement on modern business and society.

parts.content: To what extent are the characteristics of a well-designed algorithm critical for ensuring accuracy and reliability in healthcare diagnostics?
parts.markscheme:
Candidates are expected to explore the importance of well-designed algorithms in ensuring accuracy and reliability in healthcare diagnostics. Possible points to consider include:

- Accuracy and reliability are critical in healthcare diagnostics as errors can have severe consequences for patient health and well-being. <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Well-designed algorithms should have clear and unambiguous logic, with no room for misinterpretation or errors. This ensures consistent and reliable outputs. <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Algorithms should be thoroughly tested and validated against known cases to ensure they produce accurate results. <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Algorithms should be designed to handle edge cases and exceptions gracefully, without producing erroneous outputs. <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Algorithms should be designed with appropriate data validation and error handling mechanisms to ensure that input data is clean and within expected ranges. <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Algorithms should be designed with transparency and explainability in mind, allowing healthcare professionals to understand the reasoning behind the diagnostic outputs. <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Well-designed algorithms should be regularly updated and maintained to incorporate new medical knowledge and data, ensuring they remain accurate and relevant. <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Algorithms should be designed with privacy and security considerations in mind, protecting sensitive patient data. <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Algorithms should be designed with scalability and performance in mind, ensuring they can handle large volumes of data and provide timely results. <marks>1 mark</marks>
